title: 17776 by Jon Bois
verdict: Shortlist-borderline: the self-exemplifying framing and the creative-format-inertia angle are genuinely interesting, but the long middle digression loses focus on the book itself and the payoff arrives later than it should.
strongest: The closing observation that 17776 is self-exemplifying — a work in a new format about creative expression through new formats — ties the whole review together in a genuinely insightful way.
weakest: The extended four-part analysis of why multimedia fiction hasn't gone mainstream, while interesting in isolation, drifts too far from the book and reads more like a personal essay tangent than a review.

title: 1984 by George Orwell
verdict: Do not shortlist: the review displays genuine learning and occasional sharp observations (the Burnham managerial-class angle, the Buddhist epistemology parallel, the Tolkien comparison) but is dragged down by an overlong biographical section, well-worn "Orwellian predictions came true" framing, and a closing checklist of contemporary examples that adds length without adding thought.
strongest: The reading of the Party as a proto-PMC oligarchy drawing on Burnham's managerial revolution thesis is a substantive, non-obvious intellectual move that briefly elevates the review above standard Orwell commentary.
weakest: The extensive biographical middle section reads as conventional background filler, and the final paragraph's list of present-day Orwellian parallels is exactly the kind of low-effort "the author was right about everything" conclusion that substitutes political agreement for critical thought.

title: A Christmas Carol by Charles Dickens
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the review advances a genuinely non-obvious thesis (Scrooge fears dying unmourned, not dying), supports it rigorously with the text, and delivers it in a warm, confident voice that earns its length.
strongest: The central argument - that the Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come deliberately conceals the body's identity to bypass motivated reasoning, and that Scrooge's revelation is about dying without impact rather than dying at all - is a textually grounded, genuinely insightful reframe that reshapes how you read the book.
weakest: The song-lyric brackets and the extended APT digression in footnote 7 slightly diffuse the essay's focus, and the closing call to "honor Christmas" edges toward the sentimental without fully earning it analytically.

title: A Game of Thrones (A Song of Ice and Fire, Book 1) by George R.R. Martin
verdict: Do not shortlist: the review is almost entirely plot summary with a casual voice but no real thesis, argument, or non-obvious insight to reward.
strongest: The conversational, self-aware voice has occasional wit (the "-10 years old" joke, calling GRRM "Samuel Tarly") that gives the piece some personality.
weakest: The review is essentially a plot synopsis with no sustained argument or original angle — the one gesture toward a thesis (the book is "real and brutal" because characters make internally coherent choices) is never developed or defended.

title: A History of the Jews by Paul Johnson
verdict: Do not shortlist: the Polish-outsider-meets-rationalist-community angle is genuinely interesting but never develops into a thesis, leaving the piece as an annotated reading log rather than a review.
strongest: The framing of a non-Jewish Pole reflecting on how Jewish culture invisibly shaped his intellectual life — via Yudkowsky, LessWrong, and his country's history — is an original and promising angle that no other reviewer is likely to share.
weakest: The review explicitly abandons its own structure in favor of a chronological dump of highlighted passages, so the intriguing opening thesis about Jewish rationalism and the ACX community is never developed, argued, or paid off.

title: A Poor Wise Man by Mary Roberts Rinehart
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: a clever, well-executed review that uses a forgotten 1920 bestseller to illuminate the original Red Scare and its mainstream American attitudes, with a strong framing device and a payoff ending that earns the read.
strongest: The opening hypothetical — transposing a 1920 bestselling vigilante romance onto 2020 — is a genuinely original framing device that instantly makes the historical argument concrete and compelling.
weakest: The middle character-sketch sections (particularly Heroine and Moderate Republican) run slightly long and shade toward plot summary, diluting the momentum built by the strong opening.

title: A Residence of Twenty-One Years in the Sandwich Islands
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the EA/missionary parallel is a genuinely original and well-executed thesis, the personal family connection gives it unusual authenticity, and the historical detail is rich enough to earn the length.
strongest: The structural parallel between 19th-century missionary logic and Effective Altruism — applied with specific analytical vocabulary (neglectedness, tractability, cost-effectiveness) rather than merely asserted — is a genuinely fresh insight that reframes both subjects.
weakest: The closing "lessons learned" section collapses into a somewhat generic listicle of leadership maxims that undersells the more interesting tensions the review had been building throughout.

title: A Secular Age (Charles Taylor)
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the personal framing and confident, playful voice make a notoriously dense 800-page philosophy tome genuinely inviting, though the review leans more toward enthusiastic guided tour than sustained original argument.
strongest: The opening conceit — narrator reasoning themselves into wanting a religion, then discovering Taylor — is a genuinely clever hook that also accurately mirrors one of Taylor's own core arguments about cross-pressured modernity, making the structure itself an illustration of the thesis.
weakest: The review ultimately delivers a faithful, well-paced summary of Taylor's ideas rather than a strong independent thesis or counter-argument of its own, so the "so what" beyond "this book is useful for thinking about raising children with meaning" remains somewhat thin.

title: A Swim in a Pond in the Rain (George Saunders)
verdict: Strong shortlist candidate — the Singer/EA synthesis gives it a genuine thesis that transcends book review, and the voice is consistently alive, with a closing paragraph that genuinely lands.
strongest: The synthesis connecting Saunders' un-othering theory of fiction to Singer's drowning-child thought experiment and the EA distance-bias problem is genuinely non-obvious and makes the whole review more than the sum of its parts.
weakest: The three-story triptych section, while engaging, occasionally threatens to tip into extended summary before the craft-principle payoffs rescue it, and the Stalin parenthetical — though funny — slightly disrupts the argumentative momentum early on.

title: A World Appears by Michael Pollan
verdict: Do not shortlist: this is an unfinished draft — three of four major sections exist only as bullet-point outlines with placeholder notes, making it impossible to evaluate as a complete review.
strongest: The completed "Sentience" section shows genuine intellectual curiosity, tracing a coherent thread from plant intelligence through Friston's free-energy principle to the question of why phenomenal consciousness exists at all if goal-directed behavior doesn't require it.
weakest: The review is structurally incomplete: the "Feeling," "Thinking," and "Self" chapters are nothing but skeletal outlines with stub labels and editorial placeholders like "[insert]", rendering most of the promised analysis absent.

title: Aesopian Language in Russian Literature (Lev Loseff / Alexandra Arkhipova)
verdict: Strong shortlist candidate: the review advances a genuine thesis about Aesopian as both diagnostic tool and survival skill, teaches through well-chosen examples, and earns its length with a productive final section that interrogates the method's epistemological limits.
strongest: The epistemological challenge in Section 5 — honestly confronting the unfalsifiability of Aesopian readings and then arguing that unfalsifiability is the mechanism's feature, not a flaw — is genuinely non-obvious and analytically satisfying.
weakest: The LLM experiment section is tantalizing but left incomplete, with the actual GPT-5.5 responses described as placeholders ("This is GPT-5.5's response after I fed it…") rather than quoted, which deflates a promising empirical digression.

title: Agatha Christie's Works
verdict: Shortlist-worthy but not quite finalist material — it makes genuine arguments about Christie's craft and ethics with confidence and voice, but the core aesthetic defense (apparent weaknesses are actually purposive strengths) is a recognizable position rather than a truly fresh take.
strongest: The ethics section, particularly the argument that Christie's appeal lies in a world governed by unquestioned rules where evil is treated as an anomaly rather than a constant condition, offers a genuinely interesting and well-supported thesis that most Christie defenses don't articulate this clearly.
weakest: The aesthetic argument, while competent, covers well-trodden ground — defending Christie against literary condescension by invoking purposive craft is a position readers have encountered before, and the review doesn't push it into genuinely surprising territory.

title: Alice in Puzzle-Land by Raymond Smullyan
verdict: Worth shortlisting: the review earns genuine insight by using Smullyan's own logical tricks to frame the argument for reading the book, and it has a confident, playful voice that matches the subject matter, though it leans a bit too heavily on puzzle demonstration at the expense of a sharper critical thesis.
strongest: The opening gambit — deploying Smullyan's own self-referential logic to "prove" you should read the review — perfectly embodies the book's spirit and immediately distinguishes the piece from a generic summary.
weakest: The middle sections describing individual puzzle types function more as enthusiastic tour-guide narration than critical argument, and the piece never quite coalesces around a single strong thesis about what makes Smullyan's work distinctively important beyond "it's great puzzles + Gödel-lite."

title: Feeling & Knowing: Making Minds Conscious by Antonio Damasio
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the review uses Damasio's SMFK framework as a genuine springboard for original thinking about AI alignment-as-love, but the theatrical dialogue device bloats the middle sections and dilutes what could be a tighter, more forceful argument.
strongest: The genuinely novel thesis that AI alignment requires relational empathy modeled on family dynamics — not command-and-control — emerges organically from Damasio's biology and lands as a surprising, non-obvious payoff.
weakest: The multi-character Socratic dialogue format, while distinctive, frequently sacrifices pacing for theatrical effect, with stage directions and banter that pad length without adding intellectual substance.

title: Application for Release from the Dream by Tony Hoagland
verdict: Do not shortlist: the review's formal inventiveness and distinctive voice are real assets, but it is too inward-looking to teach a reader anything substantive about Hoagland or his collection, and the emotional impressionism never coheres into a transferable argument.
strongest: The structural conceit of ending with a mock bureaucratic "reviewing body" that mirrors the book's title is genuinely clever and earns its place as a formal payoff.
weakest: The review is almost entirely about the reviewer's private emotional reactions to individual poems, and a reader who does not already know the collection learns almost nothing about what makes Hoagland's work distinctive or worth reading.

title: Ballpark: Baseball in the American City by Paul Goldberger
verdict: Shortlist-borderline: the theories section offers genuine intellectual payoff and a clever framing of ballparks as a natural experiment for urbanism debates, but the lengthy historical summary in Part 1 dilutes the impact and the piece would benefit from getting to its thesis faster.
strongest: The review uses the complete arc from Golden Age to concrete donuts to retro ballparks as a genuine natural experiment to test theories about why we can no longer build beautiful walkable neighborhoods, drawing on ideas about externalities, consolidated land ownership, and regulatory capture that go meaningfully beyond the book.
weakest: The first half is dominated by a thorough but slow historical summary that delays the more valuable theory-testing material, creating a pacing problem where the review's best ideas arrive only after considerable patience is required.

title: Being You: A New Science of Consciousness by Anil Seth
verdict: Strong shortlist candidate: the double-dissociation critique of Beast Machine theory is genuinely sharp and well-sourced, and the Semantic Calculus framing advances a novel argument, though the essay's ambition occasionally outpaces its discipline and the structure sprawls in the later sections.
strongest: The double-dissociation argument against Seth's consciousness-as-allostasis thesis — showing consciousness without homeostasis (anesthesia, vegetative patients) and homeostasis without consciousness (bacteria, sleep, pure autonomic failure) — is a genuinely rigorous, well-evidenced critique that lands hard.
weakest: The Semantic Calculus Hypothesis section becomes increasingly speculative and insufficiently tethered to the book under review, feeling more like an undergraduate philosophy essay than a book review with a tight payoff.

title: Bellum Catilinae (The Catiline Conspiracy) by Sallust
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the reviewer builds a genuine argument about agonal culture and Roman historiography's relationship to virtus and immortality, grounded in their own translation work and close literary reading, with a voice that is distinctly alive throughout.
strongest: The Mulan opening reframes the central puzzle with immediate, memorable clarity, and the rest of the review actually delivers on that framing with layered intellectual payoff — metalepsis, agonal culture, kleos, Sallust's personal grudge against Cicero — rather than letting the hook dissipate into mere summary.
weakest: The structure follows a fairly conventional arc (historical background, author biography, then close readings), which mutes the originality somewhat — a bolder organizational choice could have made the argument land with more force.

title: Between Two Fires by Christopher Buehlman
verdict: Do not shortlist: the preternatural/Aquinas hook is genuinely smart but the review explicitly abandons any unifying thesis and fragments into loosely related digressions that never cohere into a payoff worth the journey.
strongest: The opening distinction between natural, supernatural, and preternatural (drawn from Aquinas) provides a genuinely illuminating conceptual frame for the novel's theology and is more intellectually interesting than most book-review openers.
weakest: The three-section structure never builds toward a conclusion, the author openly admits there is no unifying theme, and the review ends on an anti-climactic riff about TikTok rather than a meaningful payoff.

title: Bleeding Edge by Thomas Pynchon
verdict: Do not shortlist: the ABCs framework is a charming gimmick but never generates a real argument, producing a catalog of appreciation and quotation rather than a critical reading of Bleeding Edge.
strongest: The reviewer's genuine love for Pynchon produces some vivid micro-observations — particularly on free-indirect discourse and the book's pre-9/11 pastoral — that briefly illuminate the novel's texture.
weakest: The review substitutes an elaborate organizational conceit for analytical argument, and most of the 26 "letters" are thin pretexts for a quote plus a sentence of praise, with extensive personal digressions crowding out any sustained thesis about the book.

title: Breakdown in Pakistan (Masooda Bano)
verdict: Strong shortlist candidate: the reviewer brings deep firsthand experience and a genuine thesis — that foreign aid systematically guts the voluntary collective action capacity it most needs — making this one of the most intellectually substantive reviews in the field.
strongest: The reviewer weaves the book's theoretical framework together with vivid personal fieldwork anecdotes (Afghanistan hashar, Zimbabwe religious VO, Nepal HIV network) and unexpected historical parallels (Nevius's 1895 missionary critique, Ostrom's Nepal forestry) to construct a genuinely cumulative argument rather than a summary.
weakest: The piece occasionally loses pacing in its final third, where the transition into Ethiopian Self-Help Groups and EA-targeted policy recommendations feels somewhat grafted on, diluting the tight analytical focus of the earlier sections.

title: Bronze Age Mindset: An Exhortation by Bronze Age Pervert
verdict: Strong shortlist candidate: the immanent critique — using BAP's own metaphysics to argue he betrays his first principles by excluding posthumanism — is genuinely original and philosophically rigorous, though the second half runs slightly long and the Diamond Age conclusion is underintegrated.
strongest: The central argument is sharply non-obvious: BAP's own cosmology (will as species-transcendent, intelligence as "endless forms") logically demands embracing AI and posthumanism, making his bronze-age humanism a philosophical self-contradiction.
weakest: The extended detour through Yudkowsky and the AI pause letter in Part 2 loses momentum and reads more like background summary than integrated argument.

title: Brook Farm: The Dark Side of Utopia (by Sterling Delano), with comparison to Brigham Young via Pioneer Prophet
verdict: Not shortlist material — the Ripley/Young comparative frame is a genuine structural idea, but both halves are predominantly biographical summary rather than sustained argument, and the analysis never arrives at conclusions more illuminating than "good leaders have practical skills and selectivity matters."
strongest: The cross-book comparison of George Ripley and Brigham Young as contrasting leader archetypes is a creative structural choice that gives the review more shape than most single-book summaries.
weakest: The Young section occupies roughly half the review but functions almost entirely as a biographical narrative from a second book, with only glancing comparative observations connecting it back to the thesis, making the piece feel like two long summaries stapled together.

title: Candide by Voltaire
verdict: Shortlist-worthy for its sharp central thesis — that Candide attacks the compulsion to explain rather than optimism per se — and for its Pococurante reading, though the middle sections lean too heavily on scene-by-scene recap and the ending gestures at an insight without fully committing to it.
strongest: The identification of Pangloss's explanations as the obscenity rather than as a target needing disproof is a genuinely non-obvious reading that reframes the book's satirical method.
weakest: Sections II and III function largely as annotated plot summary, with the thesis only loosely anchoring each scene rather than driving a continuous argument.

title: Capitalist Realism by Mark Fisher
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: it constructs a genuinely generative thesis from Fisher's framework that produces non-obvious predictions and actually advances an argument rather than just reporting the book, though the review's relationship to the book itself is so attenuated that it can barely be called a book review.
strongest: The three-bucket taxonomy of why people don't have children — especially the "self-auditing wounded" as a structural metastability that capitalism cannot argue with without undermining its own auditing infrastructure — is genuinely original and payoff-rich thinking.
weakest: The piece is almost entirely a novel essay that uses Fisher as a springboard; it barely engages with the actual arguments, evidence, or writing of Capitalist Realism, making fairness to the source text impossible to evaluate and undercutting its claim to be a book review.

title: Careless People by Sarah Wynn-Williams
verdict: Shortlist-worthy for its sharp pivot from memoir recap to AI alignment argument, though the structural ambitions slightly outrun the execution in the final section.
strongest: The central thesis — that "careless" undersells the danger because Zuckerberg and Sandberg are intensely purposeful optimizers for maximum engagement, not lazy Gatsby-style idlers — is a genuinely non-obvious reframing that earns the whole review.
weakest: The Taghairm/AI section, while conceptually interesting, arrives somewhat abruptly and the connection between etymology, AI alignment, and the memoir's thesis is gestured at rather than fully argued, leaving the payoff feeling a bit rushed.

title: Catullus 16 (review of the poem / neoteric Latin poetry)
verdict: Shortlist-worthy as an unusually lively and thoughtful piece of literary criticism, though its subject is a single short poem rather than a book, which may raise eligibility questions; nonetheless it earns its length through genuine interpretive argument and memorable voice.
strongest: The diss-track-vs-banter dual-reading framework is genuinely illuminating, and the connection between Roman sexual ethics and Catullus's precise rhetorical strategy (who is doing the penetrating and why that matters culturally) gives readers a non-obvious conceptual payoff.
weakest: The "Brief Aside on Latin Meter" section, while charming, largely functions as a tangent that dilutes the momentum of the main argument and doesn't connect back meaningfully to the thesis.

title: Chuang-Tzu: The Inner Chapters by A. C. Graham
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the EA/rationalism framing is genuinely illuminating and the chapter-by-chapter argumentation builds real philosophical momentum, though some sections overstay their welcome and dilute the thesis.
strongest: The sustained mapping of each Inner Chapter to a specific challenge to EA/utilitarian assumptions — culminating in the Hundun parable as "the most devastating critique of altruism in the entire book" — gives the review a coherent, cumulative intellectual argument rather than mere summary.
weakest: The lengthy dramatis personae section and the "rest of the book" coda are genuinely informative but interrupt the argumentative momentum and feel more like a scholar's duty to comprehensiveness than a reviewer's instinct for what earns its length.

title: Collected Poems by C.S. Lewis
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the review builds a genuine thesis — that Lewis's poetry is best understood as a vehicle for transmitting "Stock Responses" — and earns it through well-chosen examples and a confident, warm voice, though it could mine the actual poems more deeply.
strongest: The central argument linking Lewis's poetics to the "Stock Response" concept is genuinely illuminating and gives the review a unifying thesis that most poetry-collection reviews lack.
weakest: The biographical first section is fairly generic "here's who Lewis was" territory that delays the real argument, and the Philip Rieff "Deathworks" detour is under-integrated rather than load-bearing.

title: Coriolanus (Shakespeare)
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: a genuinely original, thesis-driven essay that uses Coriolanus as scaffolding for a novel theory of democratic legitimacy, fitness interdependence, and "kayfabocracy," marred primarily by its unwieldy length and the overlong Socratic dialogue that delays the payoff.
strongest: The "mutual information" framework for legitimacy circulation and the "kayfabocracy" coinage are genuinely original conceptual contributions that reframe both the play and contemporary democratic decay in a way you have not encountered before.
weakest: The common-room Socratic dialogue constitutes roughly a third of the piece and dramatically overshoots its purpose, sapping momentum before the Shakespeare analysis and the kayfabocracy argument have a chance to land.

title: Cry Havoc by Simon Mann
verdict: Strong shortlist candidate: the "Mark Thatcher Syndrome" thesis is a genuinely original analytical frame that earns the length and transforms a niche mercenary memoir into a meditation on identity, adventure-hunger, and self-deception.
strongest: The invention and sustained development of "Mark Thatcher Syndrome" as an explanatory concept gives the review a real thesis that pays off across all four earlier theories, making the whole piece feel like a proper argument rather than a tour through the book.
weakest: The fairness toward Mann occasionally slips — particularly the torture-cooperation criticism and the ethnic composition of his officer corps — where the reviewer acknowledges the unfairness but still deploys the point, slightly undermining the analytical rigor the rest of the piece earns.

title: Cultural Evolution by Ronald Inglehart
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the review moves well beyond summary, adds original analysis (Saudi Arabia case, life extension implications, evolutionary speculation), and its intellectual honesty about methodological limits is exemplary — though the middle sections track the book's structure too closely to fully distinguish it.
strongest: The "Open Questions" section and the evolutionary biology speculation at the close are entirely the reviewer's own contribution, demonstrating genuine independent thought that elevates the piece above explanation into intellectual engagement.
weakest: The middle sections (Democratic Transition, Case Studies) follow the book's own chapter structure fairly closely, making the review feel expository for stretches when it could be more argumentative or selective.

title: Der Untergang des Abendlandes (The Decline of the West) by Oswald Spengler
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the review advances a genuine, non-obvious thesis (Spengler's work as self-fulfilling catalyst), writes with real voice and wit, and earns its length through substantive engagement rather than summary padding.
strongest: The recursive meta-argument — that Untergang des Abendlandes may itself be a load-bearing demolition of the Western self-conception it claims only to describe — is a genuinely original and thought-provoking insight that elevates this well above a standard book report.
weakest: Section III (the synthesized Spenglerian forecast) is largely a mechanical restatement of points already made in section II, slowing the pace at exactly the moment the review should be building toward its conclusion.

title: Sowing the Mustard Seed by Yoweri Museveni
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the review succeeds brilliantly as narrative journalism and character study, with a distinctive voice and genuinely interesting on-the-ground observations, though it leans on travelogue charm more than analytical depth in its final accounting of Museveni's legacy.
strongest: The review has an exceptionally alive, confident voice that weaves firsthand travel observations, dry humor, and real historical texture into something far more vivid and illuminating than a conventional book review — the contrast between Museveni's grandiose self-myth and his helplessness over school fees is the kind of specific insight that sticks.
weakest: The extensive historical digression (Uganda Railway, Tsavo Lions, Patterson's Forrest Gump career) is entertaining but bloats the piece well beyond what the actual book analysis earns, and the final verdict — "incompetent!" — feels like a punchline that dodges a harder, more substantive reckoning with what 40 years of Museveni rule actually means.

title: Don't Bang Denmark by Roosh V
verdict: A shortlist-adjacent review that earns its place through sharp wit and a genuinely interesting insider angle, but the Ozy Brennan digression and loose ending keep it from being a true standout.
strongest: The reviewer's identity as a Danish woman gives the review an earned authority and produces genuinely funny, non-obvious observations — particularly the reframing of Danish egalitarianism as redistributing sexual opportunity the same way it redistributes wealth.
weakest: The extended Ozy Brennan quote and surrounding commentary derail the pacing in the final third, functioning more as a separate essay than a payoff to the review's central argument.

title: Dream of the Red Chamber
verdict: Strong shortlist candidate: it advances a clear, well-argued thesis about a misread character, earns its close readings, and lands a final turn — the identification of Baochai's posture with Cao Xueqin's act of writing — that genuinely reframes both the character and the novel.
strongest: The comparison between Baochai's mode of "bearing" and Cao Xueqin's own authorial posture — staying where placed, finishing what must be done — is an original, structurally satisfying insight that elevates the entire essay.
weakest: The contemporary parallel (the "reliable person in a modern office") is the essay's least-earned move, stated rather than demonstrated, and risks flattening the historical specificity that the rest of the review handles with care.

title: Earth Abides by George R. Stewart
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the autistic self-insert framing gives the review a genuine thesis and personal resonance that lifts it well above plot summary, though it stops just short of exceptional because the central argument (Ish as Stewart's letter to his younger self) is asserted rather than fully developed with textual rigor.
strongest: The reviewer's personal identification with Ish is deployed purposefully rather than indulgently — it drives a real critical argument about the novel's themes of vestigial behavior, arrogance, and acceptance, making the review feel like genuine thinking rather than fan appreciation.
weakest: The autism-as-lens framing, while fresh, is leaned on somewhat loosely — the claim that Stewart was "one of us" rests on circumstantial inference about his bibliography rather than any documented evidence, which slightly undermines the credibility of the review's central interpretive move.

title: Fairness and Freedom: A History of Two Open Societies, New Zealand and the United States (David Hackett Fischer)
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: a genuinely well-argued review that advances a clear thesis about selection effects shaping national values, extends Fischer's analysis by adding Australia as a triangulating case, and sustains a confident, distinctive voice throughout.
strongest: The "morality of exit vs. morality of managed settlement" synthesis near the end crystallizes the entire review's argument into a memorable, original formulation.
weakest: The middle settlement-colony section accumulates detailed historical material that occasionally slows momentum without always connecting back to the central thesis.

title: Fear and Trembling (Kierkegaard)
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the Newcomb's problem framing of Abraham's faith is genuinely original and the review sustains a real argument throughout rather than lapsing into summary.
strongest: The opening reframing of the Abraham/Isaac story as a variant of Newcomb's problem is a striking, non-obvious move that gives the entire review a unifying intellectual spine.
weakest: The third section on Abraham's silence is the weakest and most summary-adjacent stretch, and the closing epigraph interpretation, while evocative, is somewhat underdeveloped compared to the rigor of the earlier sections.

title: Free Agents by Kevin Mitchell and Determined by Robert Sapolsky (comparative review)
verdict: Competent and readable but ultimately a structured summary-plus-verdict that confirms the reviewer's pre-existing priors, rather than a piece that teaches something genuinely new about the free will debate; not shortlist-worthy.
strongest: The reviewer's candid disclosure of their Sapolsky/Harris bias, combined with genuinely substantive critique of Mitchell's quantum indeterminacy argument (epistemic overconfidence on a contested question) and the "how does the self actually harness it?" gap, gives the dismissal of Mitchell real argumentative weight.
weakest: The comparative structure — summarize Book A, summarize Book B, declare winner — is predictable and the reviewer even acknowledges four similar reviews already exist online, undercutting claims to originality of angle.

title: From Here to Equality: Reparations for Black Americans in the Twenty-First Century
verdict: Strong shortlist candidate — it advances a genuinely novel argument (reparations as a Pareto-optimal conservative deal), backs it with interactive methodology, and has a distinctive, confident voice throughout.
strongest: The core reframing — that D&M's own framework makes reparations welfare-enhancing for anti-DEI conservatives, and that rejecting this reveals conservative objections as performative rather than rational — is a sharp, non-obvious thesis that the author actually argues and defends rather than merely asserting.
weakest: The DEI capitalization section leans heavily on a figure the reviewer themselves calls "total bollocks," and the subsequent net-present-value analysis of ending DEI feels less rigorous than the methodology critique that precedes it, somewhat undermining the quantitative credibility the review works hard to build.

title: Galileo's Error by Philip Goff
verdict: Shortlist-worthy for its ferocious intellectual energy and genuine philosophical counter-argument, but the sustained contempt tips into unfairness that a serious judge must penalize.
strongest: The reviewer doesn't merely mock — they mount a substantive philosophical counter-argument (the perspectival asymmetry section, the "intrinsic seven" thought experiment, the linear algebra point about representation) that genuinely illuminates why panpsychism's foundation is shaky, making the polemic intellectually earned rather than merely entertaining.
weakest: The review is so relentlessly hostile that it never seriously steelmans Goff's actual strongest points — the hard problem as Chalmers frames it, or the Russellian monist literature the reviewer briefly acknowledges then dismisses — which undermines its credibility as a fair assessment of the book's real merits and demerits.

title: Ghosts by Henrik Ibsen
verdict: Strong shortlist candidate: it advances a genuinely bold interpretive thesis about pedophilic rape and paternity fraud hidden in Ibsen's text, doing real detective work rather than merely summarizing the play, though the review sprawls considerably once it leaves the central argument.
strongest: The review's systematic forensic case for the "dark interpretation" of Ghosts — working through the timeline, the pipe scene, the Oedipus subtitle, and the paternity clues to argue they form a coherent hidden logic — is genuinely illuminating and leaves the reader with a new way to see the play.
weakest: After the central argument lands around the midpoint, the review drifts into a long discursive tour of Ibsen's other plays and a quasi-sociological thesis about übermenschen as unfit survivors, which, while interesting, dilutes the focus and the pacing never fully recovers.

title: Gilead by Marilynne Robinson
verdict: A warm, literate review with genuine feeling, but it leans too heavily on admiring summary and personal testimony rather than advancing a sharp, teachable argument - not shortlist material, though above average in quality of prose and care for the text.
strongest: The argument that Robinson succeeds at the notoriously difficult task of making a genuinely good person interesting - and the explanation of why Ames' quiet holiness surpasses his more theatrically righteous ancestors - is the review's most substantive and memorable insight.
weakest: The review's central structure is still largely admiring summary of characters and plot, with the analytical thesis (about goodness, faith that opens rather than blinds) woven in but never fully foregrounded or argued with rigor.

title: Great and Desperate Cures: The Rise and Decline of Psychosurgery and Other Radical Treatments for Mental Illness
verdict: Shortlist borderline: the voice is genuinely engaging and the closing is powerful, but the review is too long, too content-heavy, and follows the book's own structure too closely to be a strong finalist.
strongest: The authorial voice is lively, sardonic, and consistently alive — the humor lands without cheapening the material, and the final return to Howard Dully and Freeman's poem gives the piece a genuinely moving emotional payoff.
weakest: The review is substantially an annotated retelling of the book chapter by chapter, reproducing large quantities of anecdote and quotation with relatively little original argument beyond what Valenstein himself already argues.

title: Harrison Bergeron (Kurt Vonnegut), with extended discussion of Atlas Shrugged, The State and Revolution, and The Communist Manifesto
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the core thesis that Harrison Bergeron is a parody of Atlas Shrugged rather than a sincere anti-socialist tract is genuinely original and argued with real textual and biographical evidence, but the extended detour through Lenin dilutes the focus and the piece runs longer than its thesis strictly requires.
strongest: The argument that Vonnegut wrote Harrison Bergeron as a parody of Atlas Shrugged — supported by biographical context, quote comparisons, and the irony of its reception — is a fresh, non-obvious thesis argued with real evidence and wit.
weakest: The lengthy Lenin/Communist Manifesto section drifts into genuine content summary and loses the thread, making the piece feel like two essays spliced together rather than one tightly structured argument.

title: Headbirths, or The Germans Are Dying Out by Günter Grass
verdict: Not shortlist-worthy — it has genuine moments of wit and the biographical revelation about Grass's Nazi service is nicely integrated, but the review largely proceeds section-by-section through the book's contents without landing a single unifying argument.
strongest: The reviewer's concluding reframing — that Grass may have written the book to interrogate whether low fertility is a bug or feature of his political tribe — is a compact, original thesis that makes the whole piece retroactively interesting.
weakest: The structure is essentially a tour through the book's named sections (The Wall, The Liver Sausage, Nuclear Power, The Dictator, etc.), which is exactly the chapter-by-chapter summary format ACX reviewers are warned against, and the thesis only emerges in the final paragraph rather than driving the piece.

title: House of Leaves
verdict: A pleasant, readable review with a fun organizing conceit and some sharp moments, but it doesn't dig deep enough into what the book is actually doing to earn shortlist consideration.
strongest: The closing "AI art" analogy — the book punishes close attention despite performing depth — is a genuinely original and well-earned observation that captures something true and non-obvious about the work.
weakest: The "villain per layer" thesis is mostly a playful framing device rather than a sustained argument; it never coheres into a real claim about what the book means or why it matters.

title: How I Killed Pluto and Why It Had It Coming (Mike Brown)
verdict: Worth shortlisting for its genuine analytical ambition and intellectual honesty, but the heavy expository first half and sprawling epilogue sections dilute what is otherwise a substantive, original engagement with the planet-definition debate.
strongest: The reviewer constructs an independent quantitative case against Pluto — original charts comparing orbital parameters, mass, and "planetary science potential" across solar system bodies — and steelmans Stern's competing geophysical definition with real care, producing genuine intellectual payoff beyond the book's own arguments.
weakest: The first half of the review is largely a detailed chapter-by-chapter summary of Brown's memoir, covering the history of planet discovery, observing methodology, and personal anecdotes at length before the reviewer's own analytical voice fully emerges.

title: How to Prevent Coups d'État (Erica De Bruin, Cornell University Press, 2020)
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the review's distinctive comic voice and genuine engagement with the book's core paradox (counterbalances deter success but invite attempts) make it a pleasure to read and genuinely informative, though the Noriega case study outstays its welcome and the structure follows the book's own flow a bit too closely.
strongest: The recursive legitimacy argument at the end — tracing non-expert Luttwak through expert De Bruin back to a non-expert reviewer — is a clever original insight that reframes the entire book's thesis about authority resting on daring rather than credentials.
weakest: The Noriega case study section is overly long and reads more as a faithful plot summary of events than an analytical contribution, momentarily breaking the review's otherwise strong forward momentum.

title: Imagined Communities: Reflections on the Origin and Spread of Nationalism by Benedict Anderson
verdict: Don't shortlist — the review is engaging and personally grounded but leans too heavily on summary rather than making a sustained original argument about why Anderson's thesis matters.
strongest: The personal framing of growing up between Spanish and Catalan nationalists, culminating in standing in an illegal referendum and feeling the tug despite anti-nationalist convictions, gives the review an emotional grounding and honesty that most academic summaries lack.
weakest: The bulk of the review is organized as book-following summary (print-capitalism, Americas, Europe, WW1) rather than as an argument the reviewer is making, so the genuine insights remain scattered rather than building toward a thesis.

title: Into the Universe of Technical Images by Vilém Flusser
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the Borges framing is genuinely effective, the AI-parallels are illuminating rather than gimmicky, and the reviewer clearly has something to say — but the prose doesn't quite reach the level of a finalist.
strongest: The sustained Borges/Pierre Menard frame is not decorative but actually illuminating — it earns the parallel each time it reappears, culminating in a clean payoff about hyperstition and consensus, which makes the review feel architecturally purposeful.
weakest: The AI-parallels section, while genuinely insightful, occasionally tips into explaining Flusser by explaining what neural networks do, which delays the argumentative payoff and risks becoming its own kind of summary.

title: Inventing Temperature by Hasok Chang
verdict: Strong shortlist candidate: the reviewer uses Chang's thermometry history as a genuine philosophical lens to interrogate LLM benchmarking, producing an original argument rather than merely summarizing the book, though the prose can be dense and academic in stretches.
strongest: The extended analogy between epistemic iteration in thermometry and the problem of benchmarking LLM capabilities is genuinely illuminating and non-obvious, grounding a live contemporary debate in a rigorous historical framework.
weakest: The writing voice is competent but often clinical and lecture-like, lacking the spark or wit that would lift an otherwise strong analytical essay into truly memorable territory.

title: Invisible Cities (Calvino) and Life: A User's Manual (Perec)
verdict: Strong shortlist candidate: the review advances a genuine thesis about ironic inversion as a shared literary strategy, has a distinctive and genuinely funny voice, and the missing-chapter observation is an original find — though the raven paradox opening and occasional length in the Oulipo background section slightly dilute the focus.
strongest: The review's voice is exceptionally alive — the self-aware footnotes, the "ugh, depressing / yay!" toggle, and the confession about the author's actual intentions in footnote [5] make it a genuine pleasure to read rather than a competent literary exercise.
weakest: The raven paradox opening, while charming, is only loosely connected to the thesis and the review's own admission ("This isn't my intention at all") somewhat undercuts the framing it spent two paragraphs establishing.

title: Kakistocracy by Richard Hanania
verdict: Shortlist-borderline: the dating-angle framing and wealth/meaning-maximizer extensions are genuinely fresh and the voice is distinctive, but two labeled summary sections significantly drag the piece down and prevent it from fully earning its considerable length.
strongest: The extension of Hanania's wealth/meaning-maximizer framework into dating dynamics — culminating in the observation that high-status meaning-maximizers adopting populist grievances are a kind of status-confused self-sabotage — adds real analytical value beyond anything the book itself likely offers on that topic.
weakest: The two explicitly labeled "Summary of Kakistocracy's Main Arguments" sections run on for many paragraphs of near-verbatim book reportage that stall the review's momentum and dilute its otherwise original voice.

title: Kindness Goes Unpunished (Walt Longmire series) by Craig Johnson
verdict: Do not shortlist — this is a short personal testimonial that summarizes the plot and expresses enthusiasm without advancing any thesis, argument, or non-obvious observation about the book or its ideas.
strongest: The reviewer's genuine personal connection to the Philadelphia setting and long reading history lend a degree of authentic warmth to the endorsement.
weakest: The review is almost entirely plot summary and personal enthusiasm with no analytical thought, thesis, or insight that would give a reader any reason to think differently about the book or its subject.

title: King Rat by James Clavell
verdict: Shortlist-worthy for its genuinely original framing — reading a formula-writer's POW novel through Nietzsche and finding that the constraints produce unexpected insight — but the structure is loose and the payoff dissipates before landing cleanly.
strongest: The central thesis — that Clavell's hack sensibilities colliding with an existentially unyielding setting create productive contradictions — is a genuinely novel and intellectually satisfying angle that goes well beyond summary.
weakest: The pacing is uneven and the conclusion is underdeveloped: after building an interesting three-character Nietzschean framework, the review trails off into a biographical sketch of Clavell in 1980 and an irrelevant footnote about Danielle Steel, never quite closing its own argument.

title: Kristin Lavransdatter
verdict: Do not shortlist: the review is written with genuine warmth and occasional insight, but it is predominantly a scene-by-scene plot summary that buries its best ideas — the enchanted-world/Charles Taylor thesis, the pagan-Christian tension, the "dogma is the drama" point — in a short section near the end, never building them into a sustained argument.
strongest: The "enchanted world" section, which connects Undset's medieval cosmology to Charles Taylor's concept of the pre-secular age, is a genuinely non-obvious and illuminating framing that briefly lifts the review above summary.
weakest: The review is structured almost entirely as a book-club-style plot retelling — character introductions, then book-by-book synopsis — with the actual thesis and original thinking confined to the final quarter and never organically integrated into the narrative.

title: Lee Kuan Yew's Memoirs (The Singapore Story / Third World to First)
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the rationalist power fantasy framing, Lee-Jefferson comparison, and "stability-maximizer not high-openness" observation are genuinely sharp, but the structure follows the book too closely and the middle sections drift into descriptive touring rather than sustained argument.
strongest: The Jefferson/Lee comparison as Explore-to-Exploit mode shift across two centuries of British imperial rise and fall is a genuinely fresh analytical frame that earns its place and leaves the reader with something new.
weakest: The review follows the book's chapter structure too faithfully through the middle sections, making it feel like an annotated tour rather than a sustained argument, which undercuts its otherwise distinctive analytical voice.

title: Leviathan by Thomas Hobbes
verdict: Strong shortlist contender — the reviewer develops a genuine thesis about Hobbes's intellectual optimism and fragile Leviathan, writes with wit and confidence, and earns a personal reckoning with the ideas rather than stopping at summary.
strongest: The reviewer surfaces a genuinely counter-intuitive angle — that Hobbes is defined not by grimness but by intellectual optimism and pragmatic fragility — and then honestly tracks how reading the book shifted their own views on free speech, globalisation, and moral universalism.
weakest: The second half of the review loses structural tightness as it drifts from book analysis into the reviewer's personal political ruminations, and the final paragraph's advice-to-aspiring-Leviathan-writers feels like a clever flourish that doesn't quite pay off the preceding argument.

title: Meeting, Hardly Meeting (collected poems of Rachel Bluwstein / Rachel the Poet)
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the reviewer's dual role as translator and biographer produces genuine insight about Hebrew's linguistic nature and the costs of translation, making a little-known poet feel essential rather than merely interesting.
strongest: The extended meditation on Hebrew's density, root-system, and the specific losses incurred when translating Rachel's poems into English is genuinely illuminating and could not have been written by someone who merely read about the subject.
weakest: The biographical sections, while affecting, occasionally lapse into chronicle mode — listing events and relatives without always connecting them back to the thesis about loss and beauty that the reviewer articulates so well elsewhere.

title: Mistletoe and Memorandums (AI-generated romance novel attributed to Coral Hart / Claude Opus 4.5)
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the "Manipulation Hierarchy" framework is a genuine intellectual contribution that transforms a snarky AI book review into a coherent theory of why AI fiction fails, though the publishing-forecast finale overstays its welcome.
strongest: The "Manipulation Hierarchy" framework — knowledge, emotion, action, belief — gives the review a unifying thesis that elevates it well above mere mockery, and the application of that framework to specific failure modes (broken micro-promises, absent voice, handshake-screaming) is genuinely illuminating.
weakest: The final third pivots into a speculative publishing-industry forecast that loses the book almost entirely and reads like a separate, less rigorous essay grafted onto the end.

title: Moby-Dick; or, The Whale (Herman Melville)
verdict: Strong shortlist candidate: the sperm-whale-acoustics pivot (sections X-XII) delivers a genuinely non-obvious payoff — the idea that Melville's obsessively complete account has a massive structural blind spot due to an impedance mismatch humans needed microphones to overcome is the kind of reframing that makes a great ACX review, though the middle sections sag into encyclopedia mode before arriving there.
strongest: The insight that Melville's exhaustively detailed account is rendered incomplete by an impedance mismatch — he could not hear what sperm whales were doing under the surface, and the organ he described most lovingly (the spermaceti organ) turns out to be a sound-production apparatus rather than an oil reservoir — is genuinely revelatory and earns the whole review.
weakest: Sections VI through IX drift into well-researched but loosely argued encyclopedism about whaling economics and natural history, functioning more as accumulated facts than as steps in a building argument, which diffuses momentum before the acoustic payoff arrives.

title: Money by Martin Amis
verdict: Worth shortlisting for its genuine thesis, lively voice, and the pornographic/erotic-as-profane/sacred analytical move, but the long middle sections drifting into Silicon Valley cultural commentary dilute what could have been a tighter, more focused piece about the novel.
strongest: The framing of John Self as Nabokov's ape drawing the bars of his own cage, extended through the pornographic/erotic dichotomy mapped onto profane/sacred, is a genuinely illuminating analytical lens that earns its ambition.
weakest: Sections 2 through 4 sprawl into a loosely connected cultural essay on modernity, Russia, and Silicon Valley that loses the novel for long stretches and substitutes name-dropping (DFW, Ginsberg, Adam Curtis, E/Acc) for sustained argument.

title: Montaillou: Cathars and Catholics in a French Village, 1294-1324
verdict: Shortlist-borderline: rich in genuinely interesting observations and honest critical engagement, but lacks an overarching thesis and reads more as an annotated reading log than a shaped argument.
strongest: The reviewer consistently applies a sharp analytical lens — flagging the selection-effect problem, tracing the poverty of doctrine to pre-literacy, and finding surprising comparative data points (communion frequency trends, folk dualism, the man burned for refusing oaths) — producing real intellectual payoff.
weakest: The review is organized as a sequence of thematic bullet-point sections with no unifying thesis, so the accumulated observations never cohere into an argument, and the piece ends without a memorable landing.

title: Moral Tribes by Joshua Greene
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the review advances a genuine argument, lands a sharp internal critique of Greene using his own logic, and extends the dual-process framework into an original political model — but the ending is underdeveloped and the reviewer explicitly declines to engage with utilitarianism just when the argument demands it.
strongest: The critique of Greene's hedging on slavery — showing that he abandons his own analytical tool the moment it becomes uncomfortable — is incisive and uses the book's own framework against itself.
weakest: The reviewer explicitly punts on evaluating utilitarianism at precisely the moment the review's central argument demands engagement with it, leaving the thesis underbuilt.

title: More Money Than God: Hedge Funds and the Making of a New Elite, by Sebastian Mallaby
verdict: Don't shortlist: the voice is genuinely entertaining and the pushback on Mallaby's pro-deregulation thesis is well-grounded, but the review is primarily a detailed section-by-section summary with commentary rather than an essay driven by an original argument, which keeps it from finalist level.
strongest: The authorial voice is consistently funny and alive — the invisible-velociraptor conceit, the Soros "messiah complex" riff, the Walter White comparison, and the Garden-of-Eden closing all demonstrate confident, distinctive writing that makes a very long review genuinely pleasurable.
weakest: The review follows the book chapter-by-chapter and spends far more words summarizing what each hedge fund did than developing its central thesis about systemic risk and the limits of Mallaby's anti-regulation stance, leaving the argument underpowered relative to the length.

title: Mother of Learning
verdict: Not shortlist-worthy in a competitive field — the genuine thesis about fantasy's "born-special" lesson problem is promising, but it gets buried under an extended plot/character walkthrough that shifts the review toward annotated summary.
strongest: The Hansel and Gretel anecdote reframes the stakes of the review's central argument (that fantasy's choice of protagonist models matters) with a genuinely surprising and illuminating real-world example.
weakest: The multi-section plot summary and character-by-character breakdown consumes most of the review's length and momentum, displacing the original argument with reportage.

title: Night by Elie Wiesel
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the review builds a genuinely original and morally serious argument — that Night's passive voice inadvertently erases human agency and thereby undermines Wiesel's own life's work against indifference — and sustains it with careful textual evidence and sharp intertextual comparisons.
strongest: The Orwell connection — passive voice as the same political obfuscation used to hide atrocities — applied to the very book documenting one of those atrocities is a genuinely non-obvious insight that gives the whole critique intellectual weight.
weakest: The early section on ellipses, while pointing to a real stylistic weakness, lingers too long before arriving at the more compelling structural argument about absent perpetrators and erased agency.

title: Orality and Literacy by Walter Ong
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: it earns its length by extending Ong into an original 2x2 framework and a genuine manifesto for where writing must go, rather than merely reporting what the book says.
strongest: The original communal/solitary × ephemeral/persistent framework repositions digital orality as categorically more dangerous than Ong's orality — not a regression but a new beast — and that argument genuinely illuminates something the reader won't have seen before.
weakest: The final "Eye" section sprawls into a wishlist of typographic experiments (new punctuation marks, mirrored letters, Substack rants) that loses argumentative coherence and reads more like enthusiastic brainstorming than a tight landing.

title: Painting Abstract Landscapes by Gareth Edwards and Kate Reeve-Edwards
verdict: Do not shortlist — the review has genuine moments of insight and an honest personal voice, but it is overwhelmingly a practitioner's diary rather than a critical argument, and the structure meanders badly without a thesis that earns its considerable length.
strongest: The Jungian digression on cognitive functions (Introverted Thinking vs. Introverted Feeling) as a lens for why the book's "emotional weather" language rings hollow to certain readers is a genuinely non-obvious and self-aware observation.
weakest: The review has no sustained thesis — it rambles through chapter-by-chapter commentary, personal painting experiments, and tangential comparisons to other artists without ever synthesizing into an argument about what the book means or why it matters beyond its target audience.

title: Parfit: A Philosopher and His Mission to Save Morality (by David Edmonds)
verdict: Worth shortlisting for its genuine philosophical engagement and distinctive voice, but the very long summary section drags it below finalist caliber — the excellent analytical work in sections 4 and 5 is buried under more biographical retelling than the format earns.
strongest: The reviewer advances a specific, evidence-backed counter-thesis to the biographer's own reading of Parfit's psychology (that the Parfit I/II transition is better explained by earlier frivolity than by mysterious mid-life intensification), and then defends it with textual citations — this is exactly the kind of original argument a great review should make.
weakest: Section 3, labeled self-deprecatingly "The part where I summarize the book and, so, Parfit's life," is exactly that — an extensive biography retelling that runs for pages and embodies the summary-trap failure mode, delaying the genuinely sharp analysis that arrives only in sections 4 and 5.

title: Patrocleia (Book XVI of Homer's Iliad, adapted by Christopher Logue)
verdict: Do not shortlist: the review is a well-curated anthology of Logue's finest passages with a cultured framing, but it never develops a thesis beyond "this is great and here is proof," leaving the reader enriched by Logue rather than by the reviewer's thought.
strongest: The extended quotations are chosen with genuine discernment and illustrate Logue's distinctive reimagining vividly, giving readers a real feel for why this adaptation matters.
weakest: The reviewer's own analytical voice is almost entirely absent — the piece functions as an appreciation and guided tour rather than an argument, and the opening Homeric-question preamble adds length without payoff.

title: Persuasions of the Witch's Craft / When God Talks Back (Tanya M. Luhrmann)
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the review advances a genuine thesis about theory-of-mind malleability, makes an interesting Jaynes connection, and earns its length through most of its runtime, though the appended AI speculation section undermines the otherwise tight ending.
strongest: The conceptual payoff — framing both books as studies in the deliberate, learnable modification of one's theory of mind, and connecting this to Jaynes and to the ACX community's own secular ritual practices — is genuinely illuminating and not a standard reading of these books.
weakest: Section VII's AI/mind-upload speculation, while original, is underdeveloped and feels like a different, less rigorous essay bolted onto the end, dissipating momentum just when the review should be landing its conclusion.

title: Pharmaceutical Freedom: Why Patients Have a Right to Self-Medicate (Jessica Flanigan)
verdict: Strongly shortlist-worthy: a practicing psychiatrist uses clinical experience to stress-test Flanigan's philosophical argument at precisely its weakest points, advances a genuine counter-thesis (capacity-based hybrid vs. risk-based gatekeeping), and ends with an observation about AI eroding information asymmetry that materially extends the book's argument rather than merely reporting it.
strongest: The reviewer's reformulation of the central question — from "how much risk should we tolerate for freedom?" to "how much instability of the chooser should we tolerate in the name of respecting them?" — is a genuine philosophical contribution that emerges organically from clinical experience.
weakest: The middle sections on the "shadow system" and regulatory inconsistency, while well-observed, occasionally accumulate clinical examples past the point of diminishing returns and could be tightened without losing the argument.

title: planecrash / Project Lawful (E.S. Yudkowsky)
verdict: Shortlist-worthy as a bold formal experiment — the review enacts the book's own thesis about adversarial cognition, decision theory, and layered ontology through its absurdist frame rather than describing them, which is genuinely impressive, though its deliberate opacity and in-group dependency will alienate readers unfamiliar with both the text and the rationalist-internet milieu it parodies.
strongest: The review's central formal conceit — performing the book's themes (adversarial epistemology, mesa-objectives, willsave failures, legibility-as-capture) through an absurdist fictional apparatus rather than summarizing them — is genuinely original and manages to illuminate the book's actual intellectual content while demonstrating it.
weakest: The review's heavy reliance on in-group terminology (snurnina, Obunga, LIGMA-9000, Baby Gronk, heptagrammaton, Zyn-as-cosmic-ritual) creates a steep accessibility barrier that makes it nearly illegible to anyone outside a narrow audience, limiting its persuasive reach as criticism.

title: Plotting Your Fantasy Novel by Jed Herne
verdict: Shortlist-worthy in the second half, where the pivot into media-economics analysis and the "selling the dream" thesis is genuinely illuminating, but the extended chapter-by-chapter summary in the first half delays the payoff and drags the overall score down.
strongest: The meta-thesis — that Herne's actual product is the fantasy of being a writer, not writing skills — is well-evidenced with real income data and funnel analysis, and culminates in a genuinely satisfying ironic twist about Herne himself being an aspiring novelist.
weakest: The extended six-part chapter summary in the middle of the review is largely straight reportage and comes close to the "here's what the book says" failure mode that ACX judging explicitly penalizes.

title: Plutarch's Parallel Lives (Tiberius and Gaius Gracchi)
verdict: Worth shortlisting as a borderline case — the opening section on Plutarch's real-world political influence is genuinely illuminating and the voice is alive, but the review spends too much of its length on narrative retelling and the analytical payoff at the end doesn't fully deliver on the promising framing.
strongest: The opening demonstration of Plutarch's concrete influence on the French and American Revolutions — with specific, well-chosen anecdotes about Rousseau, Charlotte Corday, Hamilton's paybook, and Robespierre — makes a genuinely compelling case that a 1st-century Greek biographer shaped modernity more than almost anyone appreciates.
weakest: After the strong thesis-driven opening, the bulk of the review is a well-told but largely descriptive retelling of the Gracchi story, and the concluding "significance" section raises interesting political-classification questions (proto-Marxist? proto-nationalist?) without developing them into a real argument.

title: Prior Analytics (Aristotle)
verdict: Do not shortlist: despite genuine philosophical acuity in places (the two overlooked syllogisms, the critique of modern validity definitions), the piece reads as a dry technical commentary rather than a review — it has no overarching argument, no voice, no payoff for the general reader, and ends without synthesis.
strongest: The identification of two valid syllogistic cases that Aristotle himself missed, along with the reductio proofs supporting them, is a real original contribution that goes beyond mere summary.
weakest: The piece has no authorial voice, no thesis tying the analysis together, and no conclusion — it reads as lecture notes on the text rather than a review, ending abruptly with a copyright waiver.

title: Probability Theory: The Logic of Science by E.T. Jaynes
verdict: Shortlist-borderline: the "through math, empathy" thesis and the political polarization section are genuinely compelling payoffs, but too much of the middle is a careful mathematical walkthrough that reads as expository summary rather than argument.
strongest: The review lands a genuinely surprising and personal thesis — that working through Jaynes' probability theory led the author to empathy for people with different priors — and backs it up with a concrete, well-chosen example about Trump and climate change.
weakest: The long middle section stepping through the desiderata, product rule, sum rule, and maximum entropy derivations reads largely as technical summary rather than criticism or argument, slowing momentum significantly before the stronger political-polarization payoff.

title: Progressive Myths by Michael Huemer
verdict: Do not shortlist: the deep-dive police-shooting section is genuinely strong and earns credit for independent research and steelmanning, but the review never escapes a chapter-by-chapter summary format and has a structural collapse near the end (a truncated Tax Burden section, out-of-order endnotes) that undermines confidence in the whole.
strongest: The benchmarking analysis in the police-shooting section — introducing Ross et al. (2021), framing the core dispute as an epistemological question about the correct denominator, and articulating where the burden-of-proof truly lies — is genuinely non-obvious and adds real intellectual value beyond the book itself.
weakest: The later sections (Gender, Tax Burden, footnotes) collapse into abbreviated summaries with tacked-on verdicts, and the review's overall structure is a repetitive myth-by-myth template that drains momentum rather than building toward any cumulative argument.

title: Radical Hope: Ethics in the Face of Cultural Devastation by Jonathan Lear
verdict: Strong shortlist candidate: the reviewer uses a genuinely argued philosophical critique of Lear's book to advance a live, urgent thesis about how to think clearly when facing cultural collapse, which gives the whole piece real stakes and payoff.
strongest: The reviewer doesn't just summarize Lear's argument but systematically disassembles it — distinguishing bare vs. strong hope, appropriateness vs. instrumental legitimacy, thick vs. thin ethical concepts — while keeping a vivid historical case study (Plenty Coups and the Crow) at the center, so the philosophy never floats free of human meaning.
weakest: The review's structure sprawls: the framing conceit about AI/cultural collapse is introduced in section II but not fully integrated, several tangents (footnotes, the Catlin digression, "ask Claude" asides) blunt the argumentative momentum, and the ending lands softly rather than delivering a crisp payoff that matches the sharpness of the critique.

title: Religion for Atheists by Alain de Botton
verdict: Do not shortlist — the review is almost entirely a running chapter summary with minimal original thesis, relying on de Botton's own ideas rather than critically evaluating or extending them.
strongest: The reviewer shows genuine intellectual curiosity by connecting de Botton's ideas to real-world data (the leniency/hungry-judges replication issue), rationalist community practices, and the School of Life's actual reach, grounding the book in lived context.
weakest: The review lacks any overarching thesis or argument of its own — it reads as an annotated table of contents, reproducing the book's structure section by section without ever synthesizing a "so what" that the reviewer brings to the material.

title: No Bad Parts by Richard C. Schwartz
verdict: Strong shortlist candidate — the form-content unity (a play using IFS to review an IFS book) is genuinely clever and well-executed, and the emotional arc earns its resolution, though it underweights rigorous critique of the book's actual therapeutic claims.
strongest: The review's central conceit — enacting Internal Family Systems therapy in order to explain and evaluate it — is not a gimmick but a structural argument: the format demonstrates what the book's method actually feels like from the inside, which is more illuminating than any summary could be.
weakest: Because the theatrical format prioritizes emotional resonance and dramatic arc, the review largely sidesteps a rigorous examination of whether IFS's therapeutic efficacy claims hold up, leaving the quantum mechanics and spirituality critiques as punchlines rather than developed arguments.

title: Scenebux by Cairo Smith
verdict: Do not shortlist: the review has genuine energy and voice but functions primarily as enthusiastic advocacy for an obscure novel without building a rigorous argument, and its light on specific textual evidence makes it hard to trust as criticism.
strongest: The reviewer's voice is genuinely distinctive and alive — the Luton Airport MDMA anecdote opener is memorable, and the self-aware "we are both audience and target" observation about the ACX readership shows real critical self-awareness.
weakest: The review almost never quotes or paraphrases the book in ways that let an outsider evaluate whether the praise is warranted — the "insight" payoff is more about the reviewer's relationship to internet culture than any sustained argument about what the novel actually achieves or how it achieves it.

title: Seeing That Frees (Rob Burbea)
verdict: Not shortlist-worthy: the personal anecdote opening and section IV are genuinely affecting, but the review ultimately functions as an earnest endorsement rather than an analytical argument, and the middle section collapses into a content inventory that undermines the momentum.
strongest: The opening first-person meditation experience is vividly rendered and earns reader trust in a way that makes the subsequent philosophical explanation feel grounded rather than abstract.
weakest: Section III is essentially a bulleted list of ten practices summarized in one or two sentences each — the most common failure mode Scott warns against — and it stalls the review's momentum precisely when it should be deepening the argument.

title: Siddhartha (1922) by Hermann Hesse
verdict: Strong shortlist candidate: the fictional ACX meetup dialogue format earns its ambition, making a genuinely non-obvious argument about why rationalists keep returning to Buddhism despite themselves — the tacit/explicit knowledge gap — and the self-referential ending (a reviewer explaining that explanation doesn't transmit wisdom) lands with real wit.
strongest: The framing device doubles as the thesis: by dramatizing a rationalist being slowly convinced to read the book through a conversation about tacit knowledge, the review enacts the very point it argues — that propositional persuasion is insufficient for this kind of wisdom.
weakest: The middle section of the coffee-shop dialogue becomes slightly diffuse as multiple observations pile up without the tight argumentative momentum that drives the first half.

title: Stabilizing an Unstable Economy by Hyman Minsky
verdict: Strong shortlist candidate — the reviewer genuinely understands and interrogates Minsky's framework, and the embedded critique of Wray/MMT is the kind of value-added analysis that distinguishes a real reviewer from a summarizer, though the piece would benefit from a sharper central thesis stated early.
strongest: The embedded mini-review of Why Minsky Matters, which methodically separates what Minsky actually said from Wray's MMT extensions using direct textual evidence, is genuinely insightful and demonstrates the kind of independent critical thinking that elevates this above most book reviews.
weakest: The review lacks a single galvanizing thesis stated up front — it reads as a well-informed guided tour of the book's ideas rather than an argument being advanced, with the most interesting original material (the Wray/MMT critique) buried two-thirds of the way in.

title: Storm of Steel by Ernst Jünger
verdict: Shortlist-worthy: the review makes a genuine argument about authenticity over craft, delivers real insight through the 1924-vs-1961 revision comparison, and the personal essay on duty earns its place rather than being mere padding — though the ending overreaches.
strongest: The analysis of how Jünger silently revised the nationalist ending between 1924 and 1961 — tracking his historical disillusionment — is a genuinely non-obvious insight that teaches you something real about both the book and its author.
weakest: The personal narrative extends past the point of thematic payoff (the premature daughter is powerful; the cat and stepmother feel like accumulated grief rather than additional argument) and dilutes the structural coherence of the final third.

title: Summa Technologiae by Stanislaw Lem
verdict: Strong shortlist candidate: the review advances a genuine thesis, uses a concrete modern example (the accelerometer parable) to vindicate Lem's 1964 abstractions, and closes with honest self-awareness about the recursiveness of its own framing — exactly what the best ACX reviews do.
strongest: The "Parable of the Accelerometer" section earns its place by grounding Lem's abstract technology-tree argument in a vivid real-world example that loops back to the opening sci-fi hook, making a genuinely non-obvious point feel inevitable.
weakest: Section IV on information farming and Borges's Library becomes dense and uncertain in a way that slows momentum — the reviewer's own admitted confusion ("I am not sure") is honest but leaves the section without the payoff the rest of the review delivers.

title: That Hideous Strength by C.S. Lewis
verdict: Worth shortlisting for its genuinely original framing — THS as prophecy against Modernism, not AI — and the sharp alignment between Lewis's "fallen/unfallen" and the AI safety community's "aligned/unaligned" vocabulary, but the review's excessive block-quoting and slow pacing prevent it from punching at finalist level.
strongest: The central reframe — THS predicted the fall of Modernism, not the rise of AI, and its current revival is because ASI revives Modernist dreams — is a genuinely non-obvious thesis that earns its keep.
weakest: The review is severely bloated by extended block quotations and a long plot summary that stall momentum and bury the actual arguments.

title: The Affluent Society by John Kenneth Galbraith
verdict: A solid, opinionated review with genuine analytical payoff — the AI/UBI connections and Friedman-endorses-Galbraith irony are well-handled — but it lacks a truly distinctive angle and too often drifts into the reviewer's own policy elaborations rather than illuminating the book's ideas; not quite shortlist caliber.
strongest: The reviewer draws a compelling through-line from Galbraith's 1958 thesis to contemporary AI job-displacement fears and the NIT revival, making a 70-year-old argument feel genuinely urgent rather than historically quaint.
weakest: The structure follows the book's own chapter sequence too closely, and the reviewer's own policy opinions (on taxes, DOGE, RFK Jr.) crowd out deeper engagement with Galbraith's actual argumentative moves.

title: The Bible
verdict: Do not shortlist — the review is a polished but entirely conventional take on the Bible that offers no original argument, no close reading, and no payoff beyond familiar liberal-religious consensus positions.
strongest: The prose is clean and confident, with a few genuinely punchy lines (the "survival diary" framing, "weaponizing whatever is nearest") that give it some life on the page.
weakest: There is no actual argument or non-obvious insight — every observation (Old Testament has violence, Jesus preached love, institutions corrupt ideals, read metaphorically not literally) is thoroughly familiar, and the review never engages any specific text, passage, or scholarly debate in enough depth to teach the reader anything new.

title: The Book of Abraham (by Abraham / Joseph Smith)
verdict: Strongly shortlist-worthy: a confident, funny, forensically detailed review with a genuine thesis about epistemically broken belief systems winning on instrumental grounds, though the ending slightly undersells the philosophical case it spent 10,000 words building.
strongest: The voice is genuinely distinctive and alive — alternating between deadpan historical forensics and sudden comic eruptions — and the "design spec" reading of Mormon pre-mortal existence doctrine as surgically optimized for psychological output is one of the sharpest analytical moments in any review of this kind.
weakest: The conclusion deflates back onto the South Park quote as a summary thesis, which the reviewer self-consciously admits is unoriginal, undercutting the more interesting philosophical argument about instrumental rationality vs. epistemic rationality that the review had been building toward.

title: The Brothers Karamazov
verdict: Shortlist-adjacent but not quite there — it's a sharp, well-voiced piece with a clear thesis, but its brevity leaves the central insight (Fyodor Pavlovich as Dostoevsky's shameful alter ego) feeling sketched rather than fully argued.
strongest: The Tolstoy contrast and the reading of Fyodor Pavlovich as a study in self-sabotaging shame are genuinely non-obvious framings that reward the reader with a new lens on a familiar novel.
weakest: The review is so compact that it only grazes its own best ideas — the connection between Dostoevsky's biography and the buffoon character could have been developed much further before the personal conclusion lands.

title: The Cambridge Introduction to Jacques Lacan (by Todd McGowan)
